attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department seven days after an emergency caesarean section with fever and lower abdominal pain. Her emergency caesarean was performed for obstructed labour at 8cm dilated. The caesarean section was uncomplicated. She reports an increase in fresh vaginal bleeding with foul-smelling discharge over the last two days. She has used five pads over a 24-hour period.

She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications other than paracetamol. On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.0°C. Her abdomen is soft but her uterine fundus is tender. The uterus is central and well below the umbilicus. The caesarean wound appears clean.

Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin 105 g/L (115-160)
White cell count 16.5 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
C-reactive protein 120 mg/L (<5)
Lactate within normal limits
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of postpartum endometritis: fever, uterine tenderness, and offensive lochia within the first week after delivery. The elevated white cell count and C-reactive protein support the diagnosis of infection. Recent caesarean section is a significant risk factor.

Intravenous gentamicin, cefazolin and metronidazole is the most appropriate initial management. This broad-spectrum antibiotic combination provides coverage against the polymicrobial nature of postpartum endometritis, including both aerobic and anaerobic organisms commonly found in the genital tract.

Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate would be inadequate for treating postpartum endometritis, which requires intravenous antibiotics initially due to the severity of infection and need for reliable drug delivery.

Surgical debridement would be indicated if there were retained products of conception or if medical management failed, but it is not the first-line treatment for uncomplicated postpartum endometritis.

Transvaginal ultrasound may be useful if retained products of conception are suspected, but the clinical picture is classic for endometritis and treatment should not be delayed for imaging. Retained products of conception are also often less common after a Caesarean section as the uterine cavity should be checked by the surgeon before closing.

Intravenous flucloxacillin and metronidazole would provide inadequate coverage for the typical polymicrobial nature of postpartum endometritis, which includes gram-negative organisms.

Postpartum endometritis is a serious condition that requires prompt recognition and treatment. Risk factors include caesarean delivery, prolonged rupture of membranes, multiple vaginal examinations, and retained products of conception. Most patients respond well to appropriate intravenous antibiotics within 48-72 hours.
learningPoints: Postpartum endometritis typically presents with fever, uterine tenderness, and offensive lochia within the first 10 days after delivery.
Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics are the first-line treatment for postpartum endometritis.
Risk factors for postpartum endometritis include caesarean section, prolonged rupture of membranes, and multiple vaginal examinations.
references: Society of Obstetric Medicine of Australia and New Zealand (SOMANZ). Sepsis in pregnancy: guideline for management [Internet]. 2017 [cited 2024 Nov 21]. Available from: https://www.somanz.org/content/uploads/2020/07/2017SepsisGuidelines.pdf
